page: UpCloud 1xCPU-2GB is an entry-level general-purpose cloud server utilizing the KVM hypervisor. It is configured with a single shared vCPU based on the x86_64 AMD EPYC 7542 processor running at 2.0 GHz. The hardware profile includes 2.0 GB of system memory and is bundled with 50 GB of local SSD storage. Network connectivity is supported by one complimentary public IPv4 address.

Benchmark results place this server in the lower performance tiers. The stress-ng single-core and multi-core CPU tests both recorded a score of 737.37, placing the processor performance in the bottom 10% of tested systems. Memory bandwidth is also limited, with read-only small block cached memory reaching 96.96 GB/sec and large block uncached write speeds at 7.37 GB/sec. Single-threaded gzip compression and bzip3 decompression achieved 23.49 GB/sec and 8.82 GB/sec respectively, which are categorized as weak. Redis SET operations reached 1.4 million operations per second, and static web serving throughput was measured at 1.12 GB/sec.

Given the shared CPU allocation and entry-level hardware specifications, this server offers a highly budget-friendly resource profile for non-demanding tasks. The primary tradeoff is the shared CPU model and low memory capacity, which limits its capability under sustained or parallelized workloads. This configuration is best suited for lightweight applications, basic web serving, development environments, and simple utility scripts that do not require high computational density or extensive memory resources.
description: UpCloud 1xCPU-2GB is an entry-level general-purpose cloud server powered by a single shared vCPU from an AMD EPYC 7542 processor running at 2.0 GHz. Operating on the KVM hypervisor, this x86_64 system is equipped with 2.0 GB of RAM and 50 GB of local SSD storage, alongside one complimentary public IPv4 address. Performance benchmarks indicate that this server is positioned in the lower tier, with stress-ng CPU scores and memory bandwidth metrics falling in the bottom 10% of tested configurations. Single-threaded compression tasks show weak performance, and static web serving throughput is limited to 1.12 GB/sec. Consequently, this server is best suited for low-demand workloads, such as basic web serving, lightweight utility scripts, and small-scale development or testing environments where resource density and cost efficiency are prioritized over raw computational power.
